slides.title: Introduction to Swift
slides.subtitle:
Safe, Fast, and Expressive
Modern App Development for Apple Platforms

slides.name: Aravind Govindhasamy
slides.title: Software Developer
slides.experience: Specialist in .NET, Go, and Modern Cloud Architecture

slides.title: 1. Introduction & History
slides.subtitle: The evolution of the language that redefined Apple software

list.text: Safe by Design: Swift eliminates entire classes of common programming errors by using modern safe-coding patterns.
Blazing Fast: Built with the LLVM compiler, Swift code is transformed into optimized machine code that makes the most of modern hardware.
Expressive Syntax: Code is concise yet readable, often feeling as lightweight as Python or Ruby while maintaining static type safety.

box.title: Swift is a powerful and intuitive programming language created by Apple for building apps for iOS, Mac, Apple TV, and Apple Watch. It was designed to replace Objective-C, combining the performance of compiled languages with the modernity of scripting languages. Swift is open-source (Swift.org) and has rapidly grown into one of the most popular languages in the world.

list.text: 2010: Secret development starts at Apple.
2014: Version 1.0 announced at WWDC.
2015: Swift goes Open Source (v2.2).

box.title: Swift development began in July 2010 by Chris Lattner, who also created LLVM and Clang. Lattner drew inspiration from many languages (C++, Objective-C, Rust, Haskell, Ruby, and Python) to create a language that could span from low-level systems programming to high-level application development.

list.text: Safe: Variables are always initialized, arrays are checked for out-of-bounds, and integer overflow is caught.
Fast: Swift is intended as a replacement for C-based languages (C, C++, and Objective-C).
Expressive: Swift provides many modern features developers expect, like Generics, Closures, and Optionals.

box.title: Swift was built from the ground up to satisfy three main goals, often referred to as its core pillars. Every new feature added to the language must align with these values.

list.text: Interface Builder: Visual designer for UIs.
Simulators: Test on iPhone/iPad/Watch directly on your Mac.
Instruments: Professional performance profiling (Memory, CPU, Battery).

box.title: Xcode is Apple's integrated development environment (IDE) for macOS. It includes everything you need to create apps for every Apple platform: a source code editor, asset management tools, simulators, and debugging instruments.

Playgrounds are a revolutionary way to write Swift code. They allow you to see the results of your code immediately as you type, without the need to compile and run an entire app. It's the ultimate tool for rapid prototyping and learning.

SPM is the official tool for managing the distribution of Swift code. It is integrated directly into the Swift build system to automate the process of downloading, compiling, and linking dependencies.
Swift strongly encourages the use of constants wherever possible. This 'Immutability by Default' makes code safer and easier for the compiler to optimize.

box.code:
/* Type Inference */
let meaningOfLife = 42 // Inferred as Int
let pi = 3.14159       // Inferred as Double
let name = "Antigravity" // Inferred as String

/* Explicit Type Annotation */
var score: Int = 100
var message: String
message = "Welcome!"
box.title: Swift is a type-safe language. It performs type checks when compiling your code and flags any mismatched types as errors. However, you don't always have to write the type explicitly; Swift's powerful **Type Inference** can figure it out.

box.code:
let name = "Alice"
let age = 30

/* String Interpolation */
let greeting = "Hello, \(name)! You are \(age) years old."

/* Multi-line Literals */
let poem = """
    The woods are lovely, dark and deep,
    But I have promises to keep,
    And miles to go before I sleep.
    """
box.title: Strings in Swift are Unicode-compliant and extremely powerful. String interpolation allows you to create new strings by including variable values directly within them.

box.code:
let integerVal: Int = 3
let doubleVal: Double = 0.14159

/* Explicit Conversion Required */
let result = Double(integerVal) + doubleVal
box.title: Swift provides a variety of numeric types (Int, Double, Float). Unlike some languages, Swift **does not perform implicit conversion** between numeric types, reinforcing safety.

list.text: Arrays: Ordered collections of values: [1, 2, 3]
Dictionaries: Unordered Key-Value pairs: ["Key": "Value"]
Sets: Unordered collections of unique values.

box.code:
var names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]
names.append("Dave")

var ages = ["Alice": 25, "Bob": 30]
ages["Charlie"] = 35
box.title: Swift provides three primary collection types for storing groups of values. All collections in Swift are **Value Types**, meaning they are copied when passed around (though optimized using Copy-on-Write).

slides.title: 4. The Power of Optionals
slides.subtitle: Swift's solution to the 'Billion Dollar Mistake' (Null Pointers)

box.code:
var normalString: String = "Hello" 
// normalString = nil // ERROR: Compile-time safety!

var optionalString: String? = "Hello"
optionalString = nil // OK: This box can be empty
box.title: In many languages, variables can be `null` or `nil`, often leading to runtime crashes. In Swift, a variable **cannot** be nil unless it is explicitly declared as an **Optional**. An optional is like a box: it either contains a value of a specific type, or it contains nothing (nil).

To access the value inside an optional 'box', you must safely 'unwrap' it. The if let statement is the most common way to do this. It checks if the optional has a value, and if so, makes that value available in a temporary constant.

guard is another form of unwrapping that is particularly useful for 'early returns' in functions. It keeps the main logic of your function 'at the left margin' and uncluttered, while ensuring all required values are present.

Swift provides several shorthand operators to work with optionals concisely. **Nil-coalescing** provides a default value, while **Optional Chaining** allowing you to access properties of an optional only if it contains a value.

box.code:
let definitelyExists: String! = "Apple"
print(definitelyExists!) // Works!

let mayBeNil: String? = nil
// print(mayBeNil!) // CRASH: Unexpectedly found nil
box.title: You can use ! to force an optional to unwrap. However, if the optional is nil at runtime, the app will **crash**. This is generally discouraged unless you are 100% certain the value exists (e.g., resources bundled with the app).
Swift functions are defined using the func keyword. One of Swift's most distinct features is the use of **Argument Labels**, which allow functions to read like natural English sentences.
You can omit argument labels using an underscore _, or provide default parameter values to make your functions more flexible.

Function parameters are constants by default. To modify a variable passed as an argument, you must use the inout keyword, which allows the function to change the value of the variable in the caller's scope.

Closures are self-contained blocks of functionality that can be passed around and used in your code. They are similar to 'lambdas' in other languages and can 'capture' and store references to variables from the context in which they are defined.
Swift allows a beautiful syntactic sugar called **Trailing Closure Syntax**. If a closure is the final argument to a function, you can write the closure body outside the function's parentheses.
When using inline closures, Swift automatically provides shorthand argument names like $0, $1, etc., allowing for extremely concise functional-style programming.

Structs are the building blocks of most Swift code. They are **Value Types**, which means when you assign a struct to a new variable or pass it to a function, it is **copied**. This prevents unexpected side effects from multiple parts of your code modifying the same instance.

Classes are **Reference Types**. When you assign a class instance to a new variable, both variables point to the **same memory address**. Modifying one will affect the other. Classes also support inheritance, which structs do not.

slides.title: The Core Choice: When to use which?

list.text: Use Structs for: Data models, simple state, and when you want individual copies.
Use Classes for: When you need inheritance, when you need to manage a shared identity, or when working with legacy Objective-C APIs.

box.title: Apple generally recommends **using structs by default**. They are faster, thread-safe (due to immutability/copying), and easier to reason about.

Initializers are called to set up a new instance of a type. In Swift, structs get a 'memberwise initializer' for free, while classes require you to write your own. Classes also have **deinitializers** (`deinit`) which run just before an instance is deallocated.

Swift uses ARC to manage memory. It automatically tracks how many variables are 'pointing' to a class instance and deallocates it when that count hits zero. You must use `weak` or `unowned` references to avoid **Retain Cycles** (memory leaks).

A protocol defines a blueprint of methods, properties, and other requirements that suit a particular task. Types can then 'adopt' and 'conform' to the protocol by providing the actual implementation. It's similar to 'Interfaces' in other languages.

This is a 'killer feature' of Swift. You can provide **default implementations** for any requirement of a protocol via an extension. This allows you to add functionality to multiple types at once, forming the basis of Protocol-Oriented Programming.

Delegation is a design pattern that enables a class or struct to hand off (or delegate) some of its responsibilities to an instance of another type. It is used extensively in Apple's frameworks (UIKit, AppKit).

Generics enable you to write flexible, reusable functions and types that can work with any type, subject to requirements that you define. Swift avoids code duplication by using generics for its core types like Array and Dictionary.
You can limit which types can be used with a generic function or type by adding constraints. For example, you can require that a type must conform to the Equatable protocol to support comparisons.

Introduced in Swift 5.1, opaque types allow a function to return an object of a type that is hidden behind a protocol, but without losing the identity of that type. This is fundamental to how **SwiftUI** works.

In Swift, errors are represented by values of types that conform to the Error protocol. This empty protocol indicates that a type can be used for error handling. Enums are a natural fit for representing error conditions.
Functions that can fail are marked with the throws keyword. To call such a function, you must use try within a do-catch block to handle potential errors.

Swift provides shorthands for handling errors when you don't need a full catch block. try? converts the result into an optional (nil if an error was thrown), while try! disables error propagation but crashes on failure.
Similar to Go, Swift's defer statement is used to write cleanup code that must be executed regardless of whether an error was thrown or not.

SwiftUI is an innovative, exceptionally simple way to build user interfaces across all Apple platforms with the power of Swift. Unlike UIKit's imperative approach, you describe **what** your UI should look like, and Swift handles the **how**.
SwiftUI uses **Property Wrappers** like @State and @Binding to manage data flow. When the state changes, SwiftUI automatically re-renders the relevant parts of the UI.

Introduced in Swift 5.5, the `async/await` syntax makes asynchronous code as easy to read and write as synchronous code. It eliminates 'callback hell' and provides a structured way to manage concurrent tasks.
Task groups allow you to launch multiple asynchronous tasks in parallel and wait for all of them to complete, providing a safe and efficient way to perform bulk operations.

Actors are reference types (like classes) that protect their internal state from data races. Only one task can access an actor's mutable state at a time, making it safe to use in a concurrent environment without manual locks or mutexes.
The @MainActor attribute ensures that a specific function or class always runs on the main thread. This is crucial for UI updates, which must always happen on the main thread to avoid crashes.

slides.title: 10. Conclusion
slides.subtitle: Looking ahead to the future of Swift

list.text: Server-Side Swift: Build robust backends with frameworks like Vapor.
ML & CoreML: Seamless integration with Apple's powerful AI hardware.
One Language, All Devices: Shared logic across iOS, macOS, watchOS, and more.

box.title: Swift continues to evolve with a focus on **Data-Race Safety**, embedded systems support, and even more efficient memory management. It is becoming a truly universal language, used for everything from server-side development (Vapor) to high-performance AI on Apple Silicon.

list.text: Swift.org: The official home of open-source Swift.
Swift by Sundell: High-quality articles and podcasts.
WWDC Sessions: Yearly deep dives into new language features.
Hacking with Swift: Practical, project-based learning.

box.title: Join the  Swift developer community with these essential resources:

box.title: Thank you for joining this deep dive into Swift. The future of app development is safe, fast, and expressive!
